---
title: "NFDI4Objects KG — Poseidon aDNA sites on a map"
format: live-html
engine: knitr
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
---

{{< include ../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

This browser-executable notebook queries the NFDI4Objects Knowledge
Graph for archaeogenetic **aDNA samples** from the
[Poseidon Community Archive](https://www.poseidon-adna.org/) — loaded
into the N4O KG as `collection/17` under the
[ArNO ontology](https://github.com/archaeonatural-cloud/archaeonatural-ontology)
(Straten, Strohm, Thiery & Renz 2025) — aggregates them by discovery
site, and plots the result on two interactive
[Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/) maps. The first map shows
individual sites as country-coloured markers; the second shows the
same sites styled by sample count, so concentrations of heavily
sampled locations are visible at a glance.

It is part of an Open Educational Resource series on knowledge
graphs and linked open data, and is designed to stand on its own. A
local-Python variant of this notebook is available as
`n4okg-poseidon-sites-map.ipynb`.

::: {.callout-note}
On first load, your browser downloads the Python runtime (Pyodide,
~10 MB) and the Leaflet library (~150 KB). Please allow a moment for
these to initialise.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
The NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph is a research prototype. If this
notebook fails to load data with a *network error*, the endpoint may
be temporarily unreachable or may not allow cross-origin browser
requests from this page's domain. The local `.ipynb` companion is
not affected by this and is always a reliable fallback.
:::

## About this notebook

### Why this dataset?

`collection/17` holds the Poseidon Community Archive (PCA), a large
open corpus of archaeogenetic genotype data with contextual and
bibliographic metadata (Schmid et al. 2024). For teaching purposes
it offers something most linked-data-for-archaeology examples do
not: a **natural-science dataset** mapped into a cultural-heritage
knowledge graph. Samples come with coordinates, countries, dating,
capture types, and genetic measurements — enough structure to make
meaningful aggregations visible without being overwhelming.

### What you'll learn

- how to traverse a multi-hop ArNO path
  (`aDNASample → DiscoverySite → Site → Place → Country`) in a
  single SPARQL query
- how the `GeoSPARQL` two-step indirection works on this endpoint
  (geometry is attached to the `DiscoverySite`, not to the `Sample`)
- how to aggregate by a coordinate tuple so that one named site with
  multiple samples collapses to a single visible marker

### Data-context notes

- **Coordinates live on the `DiscoverySite`, not on the `Sample`.**
  The ArNO modelling keeps the sample focused on what *is the
  sample* (tissue, genetics, dating) and delegates *where it was
  found* to the discovery site. The query path reflects this:
  `?sample arno:foundAtDiscoverySite ?ds . ?ds geo:hasGeometry/geo:asWKT ?wkt`.
- **Aggregation is by `(site, country, wkt)`** rather than by site
  name alone. A single named site may in principle be represented
  by more than one coordinate in the underlying data, and grouping
  by the geometry tuple avoids accidentally unifying them. The
  poseidon2lod README also notes that site and location reference
  coordinates are created by averaging associated discovery-site
  coordinates when matching against GeoNames and Wikidata.
- **WKT literals** on this endpoint use `POINT(lon lat)` (uppercase,
  longitude first). The parser below handles both casings
  defensively and swaps the order for Leaflet.

### Tooling notes

In the browser, SPARQL access goes through `pyodide.http.pyfetch` —
`SPARQLWrapper` is not available in Pyodide (it depends on
`requests`, which requires a system-level networking stack absent in
WebAssembly). Mapping uses a hand-rolled Leaflet block returned via
`_repr_html_` rather than `folium`, because `folium` writes its
output to disk and relies on file-system paths that Pyodide cannot
expose to the parent page.

## Step 1 — Define the SPARQL query

The query walks the ArNO path
`aDNASample → foundAtDiscoverySite → atSite → atPlace → inCountry`
to reach each sample's country, and in parallel pulls the WKT
geometry from the discovery site via `geo:hasGeometry/geo:asWKT`.
We then `GROUP BY` site label, country label, and WKT so that one
row of the result is one *place on the map*, regardless of how many
samples it represents.

```{pyodide}
#| label: setup
#| autorun: false
from pyodide.http import pyfetch
import urllib.parse
import re
import json
import pandas as pd

SPARQL_ENDPOINT = "https://graph.nfdi4objects.net/api/sparql"

SPARQL_QUERY = """
PREFIX arno: <http://archaeonatural.cloud/ont/>
PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX geosparql: <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>
SELECT ?siteLabel ?countryLabel ?wkt (COUNT(DISTINCT ?sample) AS ?numSamples)
WHERE {
  GRAPH <https://graph.nfdi4objects.net/collection/17> {
    ?sample rdf:type arno:aDNASample ;
            arno:foundAtDiscoverySite ?discoverySite .
    ?discoverySite geosparql:hasGeometry ?geom .
    ?geom geosparql:asWKT ?wkt .
    OPTIONAL {
      ?discoverySite arno:atSite ?site .
      ?site rdfs:label ?siteLabel .
    }
    OPTIONAL {
      ?discoverySite arno:atSite ?site .
      ?site arno:atPlace ?place .
      ?place arno:inCountry ?country .
      ?country rdfs:label ?countryLabel .
    }
  }
}
GROUP BY ?siteLabel ?countryLabel ?wkt
ORDER BY DESC(?numSamples)
"""

async def fetch_sparql(endpoint, query):
    # POST with form-encoded body is the SPARQL 1.1 standard transport
    # for long queries and the most robust option across endpoints that
    # use GRAPH clauses with long IRIs.
    body = urllib.parse.urlencode({"query": query})
    response = await pyfetch(
        endpoint,
        method="POST",
        headers={
            "Accept": "application/sparql-results+json",
            "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
        },
        body=body,
    )
    data = await response.json()
    return data["results"]["bindings"]

# Case-insensitive WKT parser: handles both 'Point(...)' and 'POINT(...)'
# and tolerates an optional SRID prefix like
# '<http://www.opengis.net/def/crs/OGC/1.3/CRS84> POINT(...)'.
_WKT_POINT_RE = re.compile(r"point\s*\(\s*([-+\d.eE]+)\s+([-+\d.eE]+)\s*\)",
                           re.IGNORECASE)

def parse_wkt_point(wkt):
    """Parse a WKT Point literal 'POINT(lon lat)' (any case) into (lat, lon)."""
    if not wkt:
        return (None, None)
    m = _WKT_POINT_RE.search(wkt)
    if not m:
        return (None, None)
    try:
        lon, lat = float(m.group(1)), float(m.group(2))
        return (lat, lon)
    except ValueError:
        return (None, None)

print("✓ Helpers defined. Run the next cell to load the data.")
```

## Step 2 — Load the data

The bindings from a SPARQL JSON result are flattened into a
DataFrame. The WKT literal is split into separate `latitude` and
`longitude` columns, and we drop rows whose coordinates failed to
parse — GeoSPARQL literals occasionally carry non-standard wrappings
that the regex does not match, and it is better to surface that
gracefully than to fail the whole notebook.

```{pyodide}
#| label: load-data
#| autorun: false
results = await fetch_sparql(SPARQL_ENDPOINT, SPARQL_QUERY)

rows = []
for r in results:
    lat, lon = parse_wkt_point(r.get("wkt", {}).get("value"))
    rows.append({
        "site":       r.get("siteLabel", {}).get("value", "(unnamed site)"),
        "country":    r.get("countryLabel", {}).get("value", "(unknown)"),
        "wkt":        r.get("wkt", {}).get("value"),
        "numSamples": int(r["numSamples"]["value"]),
        "latitude":   lat,
        "longitude":  lon,
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows).dropna(subset=["latitude", "longitude"])
assert not df.empty, "⚠ No records with coordinates returned — check query."
print(f"✓ {len(df)} discovery-site coordinates across "
      f"{df['country'].nunique()} countries "
      f"(total samples: {df['numSamples'].sum()}).")
df.head(10)
```

## Step 3a — Map with country-coloured markers

The first map is built as an HTML string in Python and returned as
the cell's value via a `_repr_html_`-reprable object. `quarto-live`
inserts the HTML on the main thread, where the browser happily runs
the embedded `<script>` with full DOM access.

A [layer control](https://leafletjs.com/examples/layers-control/) in
the top-right corner (expand it by hovering or tapping) offers four
base layers — OpenStreetMap as the default, the Humanitarian OSM
style, Esri satellite imagery, and Esri's world terrain base — and
one overlay *per country*, each with its colour swatch in the label.
Unchecking a country hides just its markers, so you can isolate
regions interactively.

```{pyodide}
#| label: render-markers-map
#| autorun: false
assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

import uuid

# --- 1. Assign a colour to each country (reused across both maps) -------
palette = [
    "#e6194B", "#3cb44b", "#4363d8", "#f58231", "#911eb4",
    "#42d4f4", "#f032e6", "#469990", "#9A6324", "#800000",
    "#808000", "#000075", "#e6beff", "#fabed4", "#aaffc3",
    "#fffac8", "#dcbeff", "#ffd8b1", "#a9a9a9", "#ffe119",
]
countries = sorted(df["country"].unique())
country_colors = {c: palette[i % len(palette)] for i, c in enumerate(countries)}

# --- 2. Prepare records as plain JSON-serialisable dicts ----------------
markers = [
    {
        "lat":     float(row["latitude"]),
        "lon":     float(row["longitude"]),
        "site":    row["site"],
        "country": row["country"],
        "count":   int(row["numSamples"]),
        "color":   country_colors[row["country"]],
    }
    for _, row in df.iterrows()
]
country_list = [{"country": c, "color": country_colors[c]} for c in countries]
markers_payload = json.dumps({"markers": markers, "countries": country_list})

# --- 3. Unique id so re-runs don't collide with previous renders --------
markers_map_id = f"poseidon-markers-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
markers_data_var = f"__poseidonMarkersData_{markers_map_id.replace('-', '_')}"

markers_html = f"""
<div id="{markers_map_id}" style="height:560px;width:100%;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px;margin-top:8px"></div>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css" />
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.css" />
<script>window["{markers_data_var}"] = {markers_payload};</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {{
    const mapId = "{markers_map_id}";
    const data = window["{markers_data_var}"];

    function loadFullscreenPlugin(cb) {{
        if (typeof L !== 'undefined' && L.control && L.control.fullscreen) return cb();
        if (typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(() => loadFullscreenPlugin(cb), 50);
        const s = document.createElement('script');
        s.src = 'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.js';
        s.onload = cb;
        s.onerror = cb;   // proceed without fullscreen if CDN blocked
        document.head.appendChild(s);
    }}

    let initAttempts = 0;
    const MAX_ATTEMPTS = 40;

    function init() {{
        const el = document.getElementById(mapId);
        if (!el || typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(init, 50);
        if (el._leaflet_id) return;

        const hasFullscreen = !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen);
        if (!hasFullscreen && initAttempts < MAX_ATTEMPTS) {{
            initAttempts++;
            return setTimeout(init, 50);
        }}

        // --- Base layers -----------------------------------------------
        const osm = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors'
        }});
        const osmHOT = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; OpenStreetMap contributors, Tiles style by <a href="https://www.hotosm.org/" target="_blank">HOT</a> '
                + 'hosted by <a href="https://openstreetmap.fr/" target="_blank">OSM France</a>'
        }});
        const esriImagery = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 18,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Sources: Esri, Maxar, Earthstar Geographics, and the GIS User Community'
        }});
        const esriTerrain = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Terrain_Base/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 13,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: USGS, Esri, TANA, DeLorme, and NPS'
        }});

        // --- Overlays: one layer per country --------------------------
        const countryLayers = {{}};
        const allBounds = [];
        for (const c of data.countries) {{
            countryLayers[c.country] = L.layerGroup();
        }}
        for (const m of data.markers) {{
            const marker = L.circleMarker([m.lat, m.lon], {{
                radius: 6, color: m.color, weight: 1.2,
                fillColor: m.color, fillOpacity: 0.8
            }}).bindPopup(
                '<b>' + m.site + '</b><br>' +
                'Country: ' + m.country + '<br>' +
                'aDNA samples: <b>' + m.count + '</b>'
            );
            marker.addTo(countryLayers[m.country]);
            allBounds.push([m.lat, m.lon]);
        }}

        const initialLayers = [osm];
        const overlays = {{}};
        for (const c of data.countries) {{
            initialLayers.push(countryLayers[c.country]);
            const swatch = '<span style="display:inline-block;width:10px;height:10px;'
                + 'background:' + c.color + ';border-radius:50%;'
                + 'margin-right:6px;vertical-align:middle"></span>';
            overlays[swatch + c.country] = countryLayers[c.country];
        }}

        const mapOptions = {{
            center: [30.0, 10.0], zoom: 2,
            layers: initialLayers
        }};
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            mapOptions.fullscreenControl = true;
            mapOptions.fullscreenControlOptions = {{ position: 'topleft' }};
        }}
        const map = L.map(mapId, mapOptions);
        if (allBounds.length) map.fitBounds(allBounds, {{ padding: [30, 30] }});

        const baseLayers = {{
            "OpenStreetMap": osm,
            "OpenStreetMap.HOT": osmHOT,
            "Esri.WorldImagery": esriImagery,
            "Esri.WorldTerrain": esriTerrain
        }};
        L.control.layers(baseLayers, overlays, {{ collapsed: true }}).addTo(map);

        setTimeout(() => map.invalidateSize(), 200);
    }}

    loadFullscreenPlugin(init);
}})();
</script>
"""

class _Html:
    def __init__(self, s): self._s = s
    def _repr_html_(self): return self._s

_Html(markers_html)
```

## Step 3b — Hex-binned sample density

The second map aggregates samples into a **hexagonal grid**, with
each cell coloured by the number of aDNA samples falling inside
it. A site with hundreds of samples contributes *hundreds* to its
cell, not one — so the heatmap reflects **sampling intensity**,
not just the presence of a dig. Overlapping sites in the same
region compound, which makes heavily-studied clusters (Central
Europe, the Eurasian steppe, Anatolia) jump out even at a world
zoom level.

We hand-roll the hex binning in Python and draw the polygons as
Leaflet `L.polygon` — no extra plugin, no canvas resize quirks, and
the colour legend aligns perfectly with the underlying buckets.
The hex size is ~1.0° longitude (roughly 80 km at 45°N), large
enough to aggregate multiple neighbouring sites into each cell, and
small enough to keep regional structure visible.

```{pyodide}
#| label: render-hex-map
#| autorun: false
import math
from collections import Counter

assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

# Weight each site by its sample count: one site with 341 samples
# contributes 341 to its hex cell. This is what makes the density map
# a *sampling-intensity* map rather than a site-count map.
weighted_points = [
    (float(row["longitude"]), float(row["latitude"]), int(row["numSamples"]))
    for _, row in df.iterrows()
]

# ~1.0° ≈ 80 km at 45°N. With a world-spanning dataset this gives
# roughly 100–200 non-empty cells, which is enough regional detail
# without cluttering the map.
HEX_SIZE_DEG = 1.0
phi0 = math.radians(df["latitude"].mean())
ky   = 1.0 / math.cos(phi0)

def point_to_hex(lon, lat, size):
    x, y = lon, lat * ky
    q = (2/3) * x / size
    r = (-1/3) * x / size + (math.sqrt(3)/3) * y / size
    cx, cz = q, r; cy = -cx - cz
    rx, ry, rz = round(cx), round(cy), round(cz)
    dx, dy, dz = abs(rx - cx), abs(ry - cy), abs(rz - cz)
    if dx > dy and dx > dz:   rx = -ry - rz
    elif dy > dz:             ry = -rx - rz
    else:                     rz = -rx - ry
    return int(rx), int(rz)

def hex_polygon(q, r, size):
    cx = size * 1.5 * q
    cy = size * math.sqrt(3) * (r + q/2)
    return [((cy + size * math.sin(math.radians(60*i))) / ky,
              cx + size * math.cos(math.radians(60*i))) for i in range(6)]

# Accumulate sample counts per hex cell (not site counts).
counts = Counter()
for lon, lat, n in weighted_points:
    counts[point_to_hex(lon, lat, HEX_SIZE_DEG)] += n

if not counts:
    raise RuntimeError("No hex cells produced — did the query return coordinates?")

max_n = max(counts.values())
# Five-step viridis ramp, dark purple (low) → yellow (high), consistent
# with the proportional-symbol conventions of other notebooks in this OER.
VIRIDIS = ["#440154", "#3b528b", "#21908d", "#5dc863", "#fde725"]
N_BINS  = len(VIRIDIS)

def bucket_colour(n):
    # Log-scaled bucketing — aDNA sample counts per cell are heavily
    # right-skewed (a few cells in the hundreds, many in the single
    # digits). Linear bins would collapse most of the map into one
    # colour; log bins keep small clusters visible.
    t = math.log(n) / math.log(max_n) if max_n > 1 else 1.0
    idx = min(N_BINS - 1, int(t * N_BINS))
    return VIRIDIS[idx]

# Bin edges for the legend — back out from the log bucketing.
legend_items = []
if max_n > 1:
    for i in range(N_BINS):
        lo_t = i       / N_BINS
        hi_t = (i + 1) / N_BINS
        lo = max(1, int(math.ceil(math.exp(lo_t * math.log(max_n)))))
        hi = max(lo, int(math.floor(math.exp(hi_t * math.log(max_n)))))
        legend_items.append({
            "colour": VIRIDIS[i],
            "label":  f"{lo}" if lo == hi else f"{lo}–{hi}",
        })
else:
    legend_items.append({"colour": VIRIDIS[-1], "label": "1"})

features = [{
    "polygon": hex_polygon(q, r, HEX_SIZE_DEG),
    "count":   int(n),
    "color":   bucket_colour(n),
} for (q, r), n in counts.items()]

print(f"✓ {len(features)} non-empty hex cells "
      f"(max samples per cell: {max_n:,}; total: {sum(counts.values()):,})")

count_payload = json.dumps({
    "features":   features,
    "legend":     legend_items,
    "centre":     [float(df["latitude"].mean()), float(df["longitude"].mean())],
    "max_count":  max_n,
})

count_map_id = f"poseidon-hex-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
count_data_var = f"__poseidonHexData_{count_map_id.replace('-', '_')}"

count_html = f"""
<div id="{count_map_id}" style="height:560px;width:100%;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px;margin-top:8px;position:relative"></div>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css" />
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.css" />
<script>window["{count_data_var}"] = {count_payload};</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {{
    const mapId = "{count_map_id}";
    const data = window["{count_data_var}"];

    function loadFullscreen(cb) {{
        if (typeof L !== 'undefined' && L.control && L.control.fullscreen) return cb();
        if (typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(() => loadFullscreen(cb), 50);
        const s = document.createElement('script');
        s.src = 'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.js';
        s.onload = cb;
        s.onerror = cb;
        document.head.appendChild(s);
    }}

    let attempts = 0;
    const MAX_ATTEMPTS = 40;

    function init() {{
        const el = document.getElementById(mapId);
        if (!el || typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(init, 50);
        if (el._leaflet_id) return;

        const hasFullscreen = !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen);
        if (!hasFullscreen && attempts < MAX_ATTEMPTS) {{
            attempts++;
            return setTimeout(init, 50);
        }}

        // --- Base layers -----------------------------------------------
        const osm = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; OpenStreetMap contributors'
        }});
        const osmHOT = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; OpenStreetMap contributors, Tiles style by HOT'
        }});
        const esriTerrain = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Terrain_Base/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 13,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: USGS, Esri, TANA'
        }});

        // --- Overlay: one polygon per hex cell --------------------------
        const hexLayer = L.layerGroup();
        const allBounds = [];
        for (const f of data.features) {{
            const poly = L.polygon(f.polygon, {{
                color: '#333', weight: 0.6,
                fillColor: f.color, fillOpacity: 0.78
            }}).bindTooltip(
                '<b>' + f.count.toLocaleString() + '</b> aDNA sample'
                    + (f.count !== 1 ? 's' : '') + ' in this cell',
                {{ sticky: true }}
            );
            poly.addTo(hexLayer);
            for (const latlon of f.polygon) allBounds.push(latlon);
        }}

        const mapOptions = {{
            center: data.centre, zoom: 3,
            layers: [osm, hexLayer]
        }};
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            mapOptions.fullscreenControl = true;
            mapOptions.fullscreenControlOptions = {{ position: 'topleft' }};
        }}
        const map = L.map(mapId, mapOptions);
        if (allBounds.length) map.fitBounds(allBounds, {{ padding: [20, 20] }});

        const baseLayers = {{
            "OpenStreetMap": osm,
            "OpenStreetMap.HOT": osmHOT,
            "Esri.WorldTerrain": esriTerrain
        }};
        const overlays = {{
            "Hex density (samples per cell)": hexLayer
        }};
        L.control.layers(baseLayers, overlays, {{ collapsed: true }}).addTo(map);

        // --- Legend — one swatch per bin in the bottom-right -----------
        const legend = L.control({{ position: 'bottomright' }});
        legend.onAdd = function() {{
            const div = L.DomUtil.create('div');
            div.style.cssText = 'background:rgba(255,255,255,0.92);'
                + 'padding:8px 12px;border-radius:4px;'
                + 'font:12px/1.5 system-ui,sans-serif;'
                + 'box-shadow:0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.2)';
            let html = '<b>Samples per hex</b> <small>(log)</small><br>';
            // High bin on top
            for (let i = data.legend.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {{
                const b = data.legend[i];
                html += '<span style="display:inline-block;width:14px;height:14px;'
                    + 'background:' + b.colour + ';'
                    + 'border:1px solid #333;'
                    + 'margin-right:6px;vertical-align:middle"></span>'
                    + b.label + '<br>';
            }}
            div.innerHTML = html;
            return div;
        }};
        legend.addTo(map);

        setTimeout(() => map.invalidateSize(), 200);
    }}

    loadFullscreen(init);
}})();
</script>
"""

_Html(count_html)
```

## Step 4 — Exploring the data

The cells below are a free playground — filter the DataFrame by
country, rank sites by sample count, or compute per-country
aggregates. Remember: one row of `df` is one *(site, country,
coordinate)* tuple, with a `numSamples` column holding the number
of aDNA samples aggregated there.

```{pyodide}
#| label: explore-top
#| autorun: false
assert 'df' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

# Top 15 sites by sample count — the "heavily-sampled-sites" list.
top = (df.sort_values("numSamples", ascending=False)
         .head(15)
         [["site", "country", "numSamples"]]
         .reset_index(drop=True))
print(f"Top {len(top)} sites by aDNA sample count:")
top
```

```{pyodide}
#| label: explore-country
#| autorun: false
assert 'df' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

# Per-country summary: site count and total sample count.
summary = (
    df.groupby("country")
      .agg(
          n_sites=("site", "nunique"),
          n_samples=("numSamples", "sum"),
      )
      .sort_values("n_samples", ascending=False)
)
summary
```

---

## References

- **Straten, M. thor, Strohm, S., Thiery, F. & Renz, M.** (2025). Data-Driven
  Community Standards for Interdisciplinary Heterogeneous Information
  Networks. *E-Science-Tage 2025.* Heidelberg: heiBOOKS.
  doi: [10.11588/heibooks.1652.c23914](https://doi.org/10.11588/heibooks.1652.c23914)
- **Schmid, C. et al.** (2024). Poseidon — A framework for archaeogenetic
  human genotype data management. *eLife* 13.
  doi: [10.7554/eLife.98317.1](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.98317.1)
- **archaeonatural-cloud/poseidon2lod** — RDF generation scripts.
  [github.com/archaeonatural-cloud/poseidon2lod](https://github.com/archaeonatural-cloud/poseidon2lod)

*This notebook is part of the Open Educational Resources of
[NFDI4Objects](https://www.nfdi4objects.net/).*
